topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard boxes sat in a haphazard stack in my living room, monuments to an impending separation. I traced the faded lettering on one, the address written in a looping, unfamiliar script. Every time I looked at it, a tight band constricted behind my ribs. It wasn't just a move; it was a tectonic shift in our shared landscape. I kept picking at a loose thread on the sofa cushion, my gaze fixed on the dusty pattern, unable to meet the bright, eager smile my friend had managed earlier that day. "It's going to be amazing, right?" they’d chirped, their voice too buoyant for the weight settling in my chest. I just nodded, the movement stiff, and felt a sudden, sharp urge to retreat into the quiet confines of my headphones, drowning out the sound of the packing tape sealing their new life away.

[story 2]

I spent an hour staring out the window at the familiar oak tree, counting the individual leaves, trying to anchor myself to something solid. The silence in the apartment had become oppressive, amplifying the dull thudding in my ears. When Liam finally admitted it—a casual mention over lukewarm coffee—the words landed like stones in my stomach. Across the country. The sheer distance of it felt vast, an unbridgeable chasm opening up between us. I picked up my mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, but I couldn't taste the coffee. My hands began to shake slightly, and I quickly set the mug down on the coaster, needing the small, predictable action to ground me. I just wanted to argue, to demand they reconsider, but the words caught in my throat, tasting like ash.

[story 3]

The afternoon bled into evening, and I found myself pacing the length of the hallway, my shoes scuffing softly on the hardwood floor. Each step seemed to take me further away from the front door, further from the comfort of routine. I kept catching my reflection in the darkened glass of the framed photograph on the mantle—a picture of us laughing on a beach trip—and the expression on my face felt alien, stretched thin and brittle. When my friend finally spoke, their voice was light, almost breezy, completely oblivious to the internal storm raging within me. I managed a weak, tight smile, a performance I hated, and focused intensely on smoothing down the wrinkles in my shirt, a small, futile attempt to smooth out the uneven landscape of my own composure.

I traced the faded lettering on one, the address written in a looping, unfamiliar script. Every time I looked at it, a tight band constricted behind my ribs. It wasn't just a move; it was a tectonic shift in our shared landscape. I kept picking at a loose thread on the sofa cushion, my gaze fixed on the dusty pattern, unable to meet the bright, eager smile my friend had managed earlier that day.
